title: The Deity of Christ
intro: In the beginning was the Word. Before creation, before time, before anything that can be named — the Word already was. And the Word was God. John does not ease us into this. He opens his Gospel with the most staggering declaration in all of human literature.
reading:
John 1:1-3 and 14 establish the full arc of Christ's identity: He was God in the beginning, He was the agent of all creation, and He became flesh and dwelt among us. The eternal entered time. The infinite took on finitude — not by ceasing to be God, but by adding humanity to His divinity.

John 8:58 records one of the most provocative statements Jesus ever made: before Abraham was, I AM. The divine name of Exodus 3:14 — the name God gave Moses — Jesus claimed as His own. The crowd immediately picked up stones. They understood exactly what He was saying.

John 10:30-33 is equally unambiguous: I and the Father are one. The religious leaders did not misunderstand Him. They tried to stone Him for blasphemy, saying: you, being a man, make yourself God. Jesus did not correct their interpretation. He defended it.

Colossians 2:9 states it with precision: in Him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily. Not a portion. Not a reflection. The fullness — all that God is — dwells in the body of Jesus Christ. Hebrews 1:3 calls Him the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of His nature. Titus 2:13 calls Him our great God and Savior.

To see Jesus is to see God. This is not a metaphor. It is the claim of Scripture from beginning to end.
reflection: If Jesus is fully God — not merely a great teacher or moral example but the exact imprint of God's nature — how does that change what it means to follow Him, obey Him, and worship Him?
abide: Lord Jesus, You are not less than God. You are God made flesh. I bow before You not as a figure from history but as the living Lord of all things. Let that reality shape every word, every choice, every moment of this day.
